hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1050mg per 100g — far exceeds the 600mg/100g threshold; potassium at 47mg/100g is insufficient to offset the sodium load
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this dish; sodium at 1050mg/100g represents approximately 52% of the 2000mg daily sodium limit in a single 200g serving, and the low potassium content (47mg/100g) provides no meaningful counterbalance to the sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrate and zero sugar per 100g with high protein (19.1g/100g) resulting in negligible glycaemic impact
type2_diabetes.guidance: This dish has negligible glycaemic impact due to zero carbohydrate content; however, individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should practice portion control given the 10.5g fat per 100g, confirm no starch-based thickeners are used in preparation, and remain mindful of the high sodium content (1050mg/100g) which can exacerbate diabetic nephropathy.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol at 89mg/100g combined with sodium at 1050mg/100g and unquantified saturated fat from pork offal
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption of this dish due to its high cholesterol content (89mg/100g), very high sodium (1050mg/100g) which elevates blood pressure and cardiac workload, and the inherent saturated fat content of pork tripe offal which contributes to atherogenic risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pork offal is high in arachidonic acid (omega-6) promoting inflammatory cytokine production; sodium at 1050mg/100g worsens RA-related tissue swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish as pork tripe is rich in arachidonic acid which amplifies inflammatory pathways, and the very high sodium content (1050mg/100g) further exacerbates joint swelling and inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sauteed Sliced Pork Tripe with Coriander (京派芫爆肚丝, Jīng pài yán bào dù sī) is a classic Beijing-style Chinese stir-fry dish with origins in the Qing Dynasty imperial court. The dish features thinly sliced pork tripe (stomach lining) that is quickly stir-fried at high heat with fresh coriander (cilantro), chili, ginger, garlic, and soy sauce-based seasoning. The rapid high-heat cooking technique locks in the natural juices of the tripe while producing a characteristic crisp-tender texture — firm yet yielding — with a peppery warmth from the chili and aromatic freshness from the coriander. Nutritionally, the dish is protein-dense at 19.1g protein per 100g with 10.5g fat and zero carbohydrate, making it suitable for low-carbohydrate dietary patterns. However, the sodium content is very high at 1050mg per 100g, which is a significant clinical concern for individuals with hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or diabetic nephropathy. Cholesterol is 89mg per 100g, reflecting the offal nature of the primary ingredient. The dish is not suitable for halal diets as it contains pork. It is commonly served as a shared side dish or individual main course at lunch or dinner in Chinese restaurants in Singapore and mainland China. The dish is listed in the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010137) under food group Meat and alternatives, subgroup Pork-based dishes.

The dish is not halal as it contains pork. The dish name in the input contains a spelling variant 'Corriander' (double-r); the canonical English spelling 'Coriander' is used in the output food_name.
